attributes.stem:
An 84-year-old man is admitted to the medical ward with hospital-acquired pneumonia. He has metastatic small cell lung cancer diagnosed three months ago with widespread bone and liver metastases.

His medical history includes major depression, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, type 2 diabetes mellitus, stage 3 chronic kidney disease, and hypertension. His regular medications include sertraline, metformin, perindopril, seretide inhaler and salbutamol inhaler.

Following a family meeting two days ago, a decision was made for comfort-based care only, and all his regular medications were ceased. Today, he reports feeling dizzy and anxious with episodes of nausea. He describes altered sensations in his arms and legs, and visual disturbances.

On examination, he appears distressed. His pulse rate is 92/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 94% on 2 L/min oxygen, and temperature 36.8°C. He has a fine tremor of his hands and appears unsteady when sitting up in bed.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management step?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of antidepressant discontinuation syndrome following the abrupt cessation of sertraline. The syndrome typically occurs 2-4 days after stopping a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) and includes characteristic symptoms such as dizziness, nausea, anxiety, and sensory disturbances including the pathognomonic 'electric shock' sensations.

Restarting sertraline at the previous dose is the most appropriate management. Even in palliative care, discontinuation symptoms can cause significant distress and impact quality of life. Restarting the SSRI will quickly resolve these symptoms, typically within 24-48 hours.

Haloperidol would not address the underlying cause of the symptoms and may worsen the patient's condition. While it can help with nausea and agitation, it is not appropriate when the diagnosis of SSRI discontinuation syndrome is clear.

Lorazepam might temporarily reduce anxiety but does not address the underlying cause of the discontinuation syndrome. Additionally, introducing a benzodiazepine at this stage could cause unnecessary sedation.

Metoclopramide would only address the nausea component of the syndrome and not the other symptoms. It does not address the underlying mechanism of serotonin dysregulation.

Restarting seretide inhaler would not address any of the presenting symptoms, which are clearly related to SSRI discontinuation rather than respiratory issues.

When discontinuing SSRIs, even in palliative care, a gradual taper should be considered if time and circumstances permit. If discontinuation syndrome occurs, restarting the original medication at the previous dose typically leads to rapid resolution of symptoms within 24-48 hours.
learningPoints: Antidepressant discontinuation syndrome can occur when SSRIs are stopped abruptly, even in palliative care settings.
Symptoms typically begin 2-4 days after cessation and include dizziness, nausea, anxiety, and sensory disturbances.
Management involves restarting the antidepressant at the previous dose, even in end-of-life care if symptoms are distressing.
references: Hedman C, Frisk G, Björkhem-Bergman L. Deprescribing in palliative cancer care. Life. 2022 Apr 20;12(5):613. Available online at ; https://spcare.bmj.com/content/early/2024/11/28/spcare-2024-005226.abstract

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
